---
title: "2.9 Implementing Selection and Iteration Algorithms"
subtitle: "Selection and Iteration"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Standard Loop Algorithms

### This topic moves from syntax to reusable patterns

The focus here is not a new keyword.  
It is recognizing standard algorithms that combine:

::: {.tight-list}
- loops
- selection
- accumulators
- counters
:::

These patterns appear repeatedly in AP-style questions.

## Accumulator Pattern

### Sum and average use the same four-step structure

```java
int sum = 0;
for (int i = 1; i <= 100; i++)
{
    sum += i;
}
```

Accumulator pattern steps:

::: {.tight-list}
- initialize the accumulator
- loop through the values
- update the accumulator inside the loop
- use the result after the loop
:::

Average is just sum plus one more final step: divide by the number of values.

## Input-Controlled Accumulation

### Sentinel loops can accumulate and count at the same time

```java
while (number != -1)
{
    sum += number;
    count++;
    number = scan.nextInt();
}
```

::: {.tight-list}
- the sentinel value stops the loop
- `sum` tracks the total
- `count` tracks how many values were seen
- forgetting the input update usually causes an infinite loop
:::

## Loop with if

### Selection inside a loop supports minimum and maximum algorithms

```java
if (num < min)
{
    min = num;
}
```

This is the common AP pattern:

::: {.tight-list}
- loop over many values
- test each value
- update the stored best-so-far result
:::

The same idea works for both minimum and maximum.

## Divisibility and Digits

### `%` and `/` drive several standard number algorithms

```java
num % divisor == 0
digit = num % 10
num = num / 10
```

Core uses:

::: {.tight-list}
- checking even / odd / divisible
- building prime-number logic
- pulling off digits one at a time
- converting total minutes into hours and leftover minutes
:::

## `Math.random()` and Frequency

### Conditions can model probability, and counters can measure outcomes

```java
if (Math.random() < 0.5)
{
    System.out.println("Heads");
}
```

```java
if (i % 2 == 0)
{
    count++;
}
```

::: {.tight-list}
- `Math.random()` lets conditionals simulate events by percentage
- frequency problems count how often a criterion is met
- the standard structure is: test inside the loop, increment the counter when true
:::

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- accumulator and average-building code
- input-controlled sum / average traces
- min / max update pattern checks
- prime / divisibility logic
- random-choice conditionals
- frequency counters
- <span class="term">2.9.8 Coding Challenge: Prime Number Finder</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain the accumulator pattern for sum and average
- combine looping and `if` logic for min / max and counting
- use `%` and `/` for divisibility and digit algorithms
- explain how `Math.random()` supports probability-based branches
- recognize frequency as a counter-inside-a-loop pattern
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
